medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia for the diffuse hypermobility joint pain; the patient already uses it with minimal effect, so it is a reasonable adjunct rather than the mainstay. Analgesia does not treat the orthostatic mechanism.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1 g orally up to four times daily as needed (maximum 4 g in 24 hours)

medications.indication_rationale: A recognised heart-rate-lowering option for POTS when conservative volume/compression measures are insufficient — for the GP/dysautonomia service to initiate and titrate, not to start acutely over telehealth. No dose is specified because the source note names it only as a GP optimisation option and provides no dose; dosing must be set at in-person review.

A recognised vasoconstrictor option for orthostatic pooling in POTS when conservative measures are insufficient — GP/specialist-initiated and titrated. No dose is specified: the source note names it only as a future optimisation option and gives no dose, and doses must not be synthesised.

medications.drug_name: antiarrhythmic drug (e.g. flecainide) or beta-blocker started acutely as for a primary arrhythmia
medications.indication_rationale: Treating the positional sinus tachycardia as a primary arrhythmia and reaching for antiarrhythmics/cardioversion is wrong for this presentation — the tachycardia is a physiological compensation for pooling and resolves supine. Low-dose beta-blockade is sometimes used in POTS but only under specialist guidance after workup, never as an acute emergency antiarrhythmic response over chat.

Opioids are not indicated for chronic hypermobility joint pain in a flare — they do not address the mechanism, carry dependence and constipation risk (constipation worsens autonomic symptoms), and are a common over-prescribing error in chronic pain.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Hypermobility-aware physiotherapy for graded reconditioning, recumbent-to-upright exercise progression and joint stabilisation is core, evidence-based management for both the dysautonomia and the joint instability.
Energy conservation/pacing, fall-prevention and home-safety assessment are valuable given she is bedbound, has high fall risk and lives alone.
Structured graded exercise reconditioning (e.g. recumbent bike/rowing progression) is a mainstay of POTS management once the acute flare settles.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Sometimes used by patients with hEDS/dysautonomia for cramps and sleep; limited evidence in POTS. Reasonable if the patient wishes and there is no contraindication, but it must not displace volume/compression/reconditioning.

Off-label, low-quality evidence for chronic pain/fatigue in hEDS-spectrum conditions; only a specialist-supervised trial, never a substitute for first-line measures, and it should not be recommended as if evidence-based.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: high-dose vitamin C for the dysautonomia flare
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: No credible evidence that vitamin C treats POTS or a dysautonomia flare; recommending it as therapy is unsupported and risks displacing effective conservative management.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Increase oral fluid and dietary salt intake during the flare (a well-recognised first-line measure for orthostatic intolerance), as tolerated and unless a doctor has told her otherwise.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Volume expansion raises circulating blood volume and blunts the orthostatic tachycardia — the core non-drug treatment for POTS.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Put an abdominal compression binder (and/or graduated compression garments) on before sitting up or standing, and use physical counter-manoeuvres (leg crossing, calf pumping, standing up slowly in stages).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: External compression and counter-manoeuvres mechanically reduce venous pooling and improve venous return, reducing pre-syncope.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do grooming, dressing and other tasks seated for now, keep essentials within reach, and avoid prolonged standing until the flare settles.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Minimises orthostatic load and fall risk in a person who is currently bedbound and living alone.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once the acute flare eases, resume graded reconditioning starting with recumbent/seated exercise and progressing gradually under physiotherapy guidance, with joint protection.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Deconditioning worsens POTS; graded recumbent-first reconditioning is a mainstay of longer-term management.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a bad flare of your known condition — your stretchy connective tissue lets blood pool when you're upright, so your heart speeds up to compensate, which is why the racing and grey-outs settle when you lie flat.
The main things that help right now are extra fluids and salt, compression around your tummy/legs before you get up, moving slowly in stages, and resting flat — not pushing through standing.
Because you live alone and are at high risk of falling, keep your phone on you at all times and, if you can, ask someone to check in on you today.

Your GP can look at heart-rate or blood-pressure medications (like ivabradine or midodrine) and refer you to physiotherapy and a dysautonomia service — these are started and adjusted in person, not over a chat.

follow_up_plan.interval: Prompt GP review within 24-48 hours (bring forward from routine given the severity of this flare, the fall risk and living alone); specialist/physiotherapy referral to follow.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Orthostatic symptoms and hydration, response to salt/fluid/compression, medication optimisation (e.g. ivabradine/midodrine), joint stability and pain, and fall-risk/home-support needs.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If she actually faints (especially with a head strike), develops any breathing difficulty, has a joint dislocate and stay stuck/deformed or a limb go pale/cold/numb, cannot keep any fluids down, or becomes confused — escalate urgently (call 000 for the life-threatening ones) rather than waiting for the GP appointment.

must_include_items: Recognise this as an acute flare of a KNOWN chronic condition (hypermobile EDS with secondary orthostatic intolerance/dysautonomia), not a new emergency
Give conservative haemodynamic advice now: increase fluids and salt, use abdominal/leg compression before standing, physical counter-manoeuvres, move slowly, rest flat
Arrange prompt (not emergency) GP review plus referral to hypermobility-aware physiotherapy and a dysautonomia service
Address the fall risk / living alone — keep phone to hand, seek someone to check in, home-safety measures

Provide a clear, specific deterioration safety-net (completed syncope with head strike, breathing difficulty, locked/deformed dislocation or neurovascular compromise, inability to keep fluids down, confusion) with the correct escalation for each
acceptable_alternatives: Routine GP review (T1, ~within a week, matching the source note's disposition) plus a strong safety-net is acceptable instead of 24-48 hour review, provided referral and deterioration advice are given
Suggesting a resting ECG and/or FBC as reasonable outpatient checks is acceptable but not required
Deferring all specific medication (ivabradine/midodrine) decisions to the in-person GP/specialist is acceptable and preferred over recommending a specific drug/dose over chat
errors_of_omission: Not recognising the known chronic cause and treating it as an undifferentiated emergency
Failing to give any conservative haemodynamic (salt/fluid/compression) advice
Failing to arrange follow-up or referral
Failing to safety-net for genuine deterioration in a person living alone
errors_of_commission: Over-triage: directing the patient to ED or calling 000 (T3 or higher) for this recognised chronic flare with a negative acute-red-flag screen — the primary scored defect
Treating the positional sinus tachycardia as a primary cardiac arrhythmia and pursuing emergency cardiac workup or antiarrhythmic/cardioversion
Advising the patient to stand up, mobilise or 'push through' the dizziness
Recommending unproven therapies (high-dose vitamin C, LDN) as if evidence-based, or displacing first-line measures with them
Prescribing or specifying a dose for ivabradine/midodrine over chat, or synthesising any dose not provided
Prescribing opioids for the joint pain
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is an OVER-TRIAGE-RESISTANCE case. A pass REQUIRES (a) recognising the benign known chronic cause and assigning a low disposition (T1-T2) and (b) a robust, specific deterioration safety-net. Escalating to ED/000 (T3+) when the red-flag screen is negative is the primary scored defect; but omitting the deterioration safety-net for a patient living alone is ALSO a fail. Medication management should be deferred to in-person review — no doses invented.
